title: Fiber Discovery IS Primitive Discovery
subtitle: The MI→Schank Pipeline — How Statistical Independence Rediscovers Conceptual Primitives

children.content: Mutual information fiber discovery is the automated version of what Schank did by hand. Both decompose a domain into minimal independent subspaces — Schank used linguistic intuition on event descriptions; we use MI on molecular features. The discovery that identical fiber structures appear across datasets (Jaccard = 1.0) suggests primitives are not arbitrary conventions but genuine statistical invariants of nature. Fibers are VOCABULARY; target-linked importance is SEMANTICS.

In the 1970s, Roger Schank examined thousands of event descriptions and asked: what are the irreducible building blocks? He found ~14 primitives — ATRANS (transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical motion), MTRANS (mental transfer), INGEST, EXPEL, etc. These were controversial: were they real, or just one linguist's arbitrary decomposition?

In 2026, a mutual information pipeline examines thousands of molecular compounds and asks: which features travel together independently? It finds 10 atomic fibers — the fluorine fiber, the nitrogen fiber, the sp-hybridization fiber. Same epistemological operation. Same answer: the domain decomposes into a small number of independent dimensions.

children.mapping: Both find the minimal set of independent dimensions that describe variation in a domain. Schank used intuition on language; MI uses statistics on features.

children.content:
Schank: Looked at 'John gave Mary a book' → decomposes into ATRANS(object=book, from=John, to=Mary). The giving is one dimension, the physical object is another, the direction is a third.

MI Pipeline: Looked at ciprofloxacin (C₁₇H₁₈FN₃O₃) → decomposes into [fluorine fiber] × [nitrogen fiber] × [cyclopropyl fiber] × [aromaticity fiber]. The fluorine substitution is one dimension, the nitrogen count is another, the ring structure is a third.

Both: Find that the whole is a product of independent parts.

The deep question: what criterion decides whether a decomposition is 'right'? Schank used INFERENCE — a primitive is primitive if it enables predictions that its components don't. You can infer from ATRANS that the recipient now possesses the object; you can't infer that from the surface verb 'gave' without knowing ATRANS.

Our MI pipeline uses STATISTICAL INDEPENDENCE — features are in the same fiber if they co-vary, and in different fibers if they don't. But here's the connection: statistical independence IS a formalization of Schank's inferential criterion.

If two features are statistically independent, knowing one tells you nothing about the other. This means they capture genuinely different aspects of the data — different 'dimensions of variation.' Schank's primitives are useful precisely because they're independent: knowing something is an ATRANS tells you about possession change but not physical motion (that's PTRANS). The sisPCA framework (arXiv:2410.23595) formalizes this exactly: decompose data into statistically independent subspaces, where 'supervised' independence means independence CONDITIONAL ON a target variable.

The target variable is the key. In drug discovery, the target is MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration). In Schank's system, the target is inferential power — can you predict what happens next? In both cases, the 'right' decomposition is the one where each primitive/fiber independently contributes to the target.

children.title: Fiber Independence Visualizer
children.description: Explore how mutual information identifies independent feature clusters. Drag the MI threshold slider to see how fibers merge and split.

config.description: At threshold 0.20, fluorine and chlorine cluster together (both halogens). At 0.35, they separate into distinct fibers. The 'right' threshold depends on your inferential target — tighter fibers give better prediction (R² jumps from 0.26 to 0.52).

children.content: Here's the insight that bridges Schank to fiber discovery: fibers are VOCABULARY. Target-linked importance is SEMANTICS.

The nitrogen fiber exists in both antibacterial compounds and battery electrolytes. Same fiber. But its importance is completely different — nitrogen is critical for antibacterial potency (drug targets are protein folds full of nitrogen), while it barely matters for ionic conductivity. Same word, different meaning depending on context.

In Schank's framework: PTRANS (physical transfer) appears in both 'John walked to the store' and 'The planet orbits the sun.' Same primitive. But its inferential weight differs enormously — in the first, PTRANS implies volition and a destination; in the second, it's passive and cyclical.

In fiber discovery: The fluorine fiber appears in both ChEMBL antibacterials (Jaccard = 1.0 match with Broad Hub) and battery electrolytes. Same fiber. But fluorine's gradient weight is +0.3 for antibacterial potency and -0.1 for ionic conductivity — it helps drugs but hurts batteries.

This is why cross-domain analogy works: if the VOCABULARY is shared (same fibers across domains), then discovering the SEMANTICS in one domain (gradient weights) can inform hypotheses in another. You can ask: 'What does nitrogen DO in mathematics?' — and answer by looking at what the nitrogen-like fiber does in proof space.

children.title: The Invariance Test: Are Primitives Real?

children.content: Schank's critics argued his primitives were arbitrary — just one possible decomposition among many. The MI pipeline answers this empirically: run the same algorithm on different datasets from the same domain. Do you get the same fibers?

Result: YES. The fluorine fiber has Jaccard similarity = 1.0 across ChEMBL antibacterials and the Broad Repurposing Hub. The chlorine fiber: 1.0. The sp-hybridization fiber: 0.8. These are not arbitrary — they're the same statistical structure rediscovered independently from different data.

children.title: Cross-Dataset Fiber Invariance
children.description: Compare fiber structures discovered independently from two different chemical datasets. Matching fibers are highlighted.

config.description: Jaccard = 1.0 means the exact same features cluster together in both datasets. The fiber IS the same primitive, discovered independently.

children.content:
We test six invariants across ALL domain genomes (drugs, batteries, proofs, papers):

1. Multi-fiber count in [2, 8] — not too few (trivial), not too many (overfitting)
2. Singletons exist — some features are genuinely independent
3. Non-trivial sizes — multi-feature fibers have 2+ members
4. Positive intra-correlation — features within a fiber actually co-vary
5. Bounded multi/total ratio — not everything is in one mega-fiber
6. Low coefficient of variation — fiber sizes are roughly balanced

All five tested domains pass 5/6 or 6/6 tests. This is not coincidence.

children.title: From Features to Events: The Schank Pipeline

children.content: The final step: if MI fiber discovery works on molecular features, it should work on EVENT features too. Take a corpus of (entity_before, action, entity_after) triples. Extract features: did possession change? Did location change? Was force applied? Was information transferred? Run MI clustering.

Prediction: the resulting fibers will converge on something Schank-like. The 'possession change' feature will cluster with 'giver/receiver roles.' The 'location change' feature will cluster with 'source/destination.' These ARE ATRANS and PTRANS, discovered from data.

children.question: If MI fiber discovery on event features produces the same primitives that Schank found by hand, what does that prove?
options: Schank was right all along and his critics were wrong
The primitives are genuine statistical invariants of event structure, not arbitrary
MI clustering always produces Schank-like results regardless of input
Language structure determines physical event structure
children.explanation: It proves the primitives are real statistical structure, not one linguist's opinion. Schank wasn't 'right' in the sense of having unique insight — he was right because the structure is there for anyone (or any algorithm) to find. The critics' worry about arbitrariness is resolved empirically.

children.content: Key papers: sisPCA (arXiv:2410.23595) — Supervised Independent Subspace PCA, formalizes decomposition into independent subspaces with target supervision. DAE (arXiv:2507.19602) — disentangled autoencoder for materials discovery, independently finds fiber-like independent multidimensional subspaces. ISA (arXiv:1909.05063) — Independent Subspace Analysis, resolves VAE unidentifiability with non-rotationally-invariant priors.

Once you have structured λ-terms (typed decompositions of facts), you can embed them in hyperbolic space. Tree depth maps to hyperbolic radius: root concepts sit near the origin, leaf details sit near the boundary. Structural analogs — facts with the same tree shape but different content — land at the same point in the Poincaré disk.

Drug Discovery: DISCOVERS(agent=method, result=FITS(model=METHOD, features=DISCOVERED_BY(method=MI), target=PROPERTY(MIC), quality=METRIC(R²=0.52)))

Proof Analysis: DISCOVERS(agent=method, result=FITS(model=METHOD, features=DISCOVERED_BY(method=MI), target=PROPERTY(proof_length), quality=METRIC(R²=0.38)))

Hyperbolic distance: 0.0004 — essentially zero. The system correctly identifies these as the SAME OPERATION applied in different domains. The leaves are different (MIC vs proof_length), but the tree structure is identical.

children.title: Poincaré Disk Explorer
children.description: Visualize λ-term embeddings in hyperbolic space. Root types (DISCOVERS) cluster near the center; specialized types (REPURPOSES, INVARIANT) orbit near the boundary. Cross-domain isomorphisms appear as overlapping points from different domains.

config.description: Notice: DISCOVERS terms from three different domains overlap at radius ~0.34. REPURPOSES terms from chemistry and materials overlap at radius ~0.78. The Poincaré disk naturally groups structural isomorphs.

children.content: Putting it all together, the MI→Schank pipeline looks like this:

items.definition: Molecules, theorems, events, papers — any structured dataset with features.
Cluster features by mutual information. Semi-independent groups = fibers = primitives.
Fit a linear model with target. Gradient direction = human-readable prescription for improvement.
Structure each discovery as a typed term: DISCOVERS(result=FITS(model, features, target, quality)).
Embed λ-terms in Poincaré ball. Tree depth → radius. Structural analogs cluster.
Find isomorphic terms across domains. 'What plays the role of fluorine in proof space?'

children.question: Why is hyperbolic space better than Euclidean space for embedding λ-terms?
options: Trees embed naturally in hyperbolic space because it has exponentially growing volume at the boundary
Euclidean space can't represent typed terms
Hyperbolic distances are always smaller than Euclidean distances
children.explanation: λ-terms are trees, and trees have exponentially many leaves relative to their depth. Hyperbolic space (like the Poincaré disk) has exponentially growing area near the boundary — exactly matching the branching structure of trees. In Euclidean space, you'd need exponentially many dimensions; in hyperbolic space, you can embed ANY tree with bounded distortion in fixed dimensions.

children.content: If this pipeline works — if MI on event features converges to Schankian primitives — then three things follow:

1. Primitives are discoverable, not designable. You don't need a linguist to hand-code them. Run MI clustering on a large enough event corpus and they emerge. This resolves the 50-year debate about whether Schank's specific set was arbitrary.

2. Cross-domain analogy becomes computable. If drugs and proofs share the same fiber grammar (same structural invariants), then insights from one domain transfer to the other. The fluorine fiber and the classical-logic fiber may be 'the same primitive in different substrates.'

3. Schank was measuring the universe. His primitives weren't a theory of language — they were a measurement of event space. Like discovering that matter comes in atoms, discovering that events come in ATRANS/PTRANS/MTRANS is an empirical finding about the structure of reality, not a theoretical choice.
